en.extract:
The Samoan triller (Lalage sharpei), known in Samoan as miti tae, is a species of bird in the family Campephagidae. It is endemic to Samoa. Its natural habitats are subtropical or tropical moist lowland forest and plantations. It is threatened by habitat loss.


Description
The Samoan triller is a bird with a length of about 13 cm, smaller than its relative, the Polynesian triller. There is no sexual dimorphism in Samoan trillers, with both sexes possessing similar plumage, a yellow bill, and white iris. The upperparts of the plumage range from a brown to a grey-brown. The underparts, chest, and throat are white, with faint brown bars present on the bird's flanks.
The Samoan triller has been observed foraging in the crowns of forest trees, where it feeds on insects collected from leaves and twigs. This insectivorous behavior helps control forest insect populations and reflects its ecological role as an active canopy feeder. Dhondt, A. (1976). Bird observations in Western Samoa.

The '''Samoan triller''' ('''''Lalage sharpei'''''), known in [[Samoan language|Samoan]] as '''miti tae''', is a species of [[bird]] in the family [[Campephagidae]].<ref name="HandbookSamoa">{{cite book |last1=Goldin |first1=M. R. |title= Field Guide to the Sāmoan Archipelago: Fish, Wildlife, and Protected Areas |url=https://archive.org/details/fieldguidetosamo00rose |url-access=limited |publisher=Bess Press |location= Honolulu |date=2002 |pages=[https://archive.org/details/fieldguidetosamo00rose/page/n245 230] |isbn=978-1-57306-111-7}}</ref> It is [[Endemism|endemic]] to [[Samoa]].<ref>[https://www.hbw.com/species/samoan-triller-lalage-sharpei Handbook of the Birds of the World - Alive: Samoan triller]</ref> Its natural [[habitat]]s are [[subtropical or tropical moist lowland forest]] and [[plantation]]s. It is threatened by [[habitat loss]].

==Description==
The Samoan triller is a bird with a length of about 13 [[Centimetres|cm]], smaller than its relative, the [[Polynesian triller]].<ref name="HandbookSamoa" /><ref name="Dhondt1976">{{cite journal |last1=Dhondt |first1=A. |year=1976 |title=Bird observations in Western Samoa |journal=Notornis |volume=23 |pages=29–43 |url=https://notornis.osnz.org.nz/system/files/Notornis_23_1.pdf |archive-date=2021-10-31 |access-date=2018-02-11 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20211031155432/https://notornis.osnz.org.nz/system/files/Notornis_23_1.pdf }}</ref> There is no sexual dimorphism in Samoan trillers, with both sexes possessing similar plumage, a yellow bill, and white iris.<ref name="Dhondt1976" /><ref name="MayrRipley1941">{{cite journal |last1=Mayr |first1=E. |last2=Ripley |first2=S. D. |year=1941 |title=Birds collected during the Whitney South Sea expedition. XLIV, Notes on the genus Lalage Boie |journal=American Museum Novitates |number=1116 |pages=1–18 |url=https://digitallibrary.amnh.org/bitstream/handle/2246/4791/v2/dspace/ingest/pdfSource/nov/N1116.pdf?sequence=1&isAllowed=y}}</ref> The upperparts of the plumage range from a brown to a grey-brown.<ref name="Dhondt1976" /> The underparts, chest, and throat are white, with faint brown bars present on the bird's flanks.<ref name="Dhondt1976" /><ref name="Mayr1945">{{cite book |last1=Mayr |first1=E. |title= Birds of the Southwest Pacific |publisher=Macmillan |location=New York |date=1945}}</ref>

The Samoan triller has been observed foraging in the crowns of forest trees, where it feeds on insects collected from leaves and twigs. This insectivorous behavior helps control forest insect populations and reflects its ecological role as an active canopy feeder. Dhondt, A. (1976). Bird observations in Western Samoa.

zh.extract:
萨摩亚鸣鹃鵙（学名：Lalage sharpei）是山椒鸟科鸣鹃鵙属的一种，为萨摩亚的特有种。其自然栖息地为亚热带或热带的湿润低地森林以及人造林中。该物种受栖息地减少威胁。


参考文献
BirdLife International. Lalage sharpei. The IUCN Red List of Threatened Species 2009.   [2010-10-31] （英语）.
